CSCE 314 Syllabus

Section 504 (58622)

Programming Languages

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: MWF

Start Time: 09:10AM

End Time: 10:00AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ZACH 590

Credit Hours:  3

Catalog Description

Programming Languages. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the design space of

programming languages via an in-depth study of two programming languages, one

functional and one object-oriented; focuses on idiomatic uses of each language and

on features characteristic for each language. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in

CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

enrollment.

Technical Skills Expectations:

Students should be familiar with installing libraries/packages, configuring

environments, executing the compilers, and running compiled programs (with or

without an IDE). Familiarity with the Linux environment is needed. Experience with

Git is expected.

Prerequisites Waiver:

Students who wish to enroll in the course without the prerequisites should contact

the Undergraduate Advising Office at  undergraduate-advisors@cse.tamu.edu , not

the instructor.

Course Auditing:

Auditing a course must go through a formal approval process per student rules.

This is often for very rare extenuating scenarios, i.e., the reason cannot be to

prepare to take the class next semester, or to effectively retake a course without

being enrolled.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the student will be able to:

Use modern programming languages effectively

Develop the necessary skills to learn and adopt new programming languages

Evaluate the power of programming languages and their constructs

Apply language constructs, common abstraction mechanisms, and efficiency

considerations

Analyze the meaning and efficiency of functional and object-oriented

programs

Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to build

correct and elegant software

Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages, learning

these languages should be considered a side benefit of the course, not the main

goal.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

[OCP] OCaml Programming: Correct + Efficient + Beautiful

Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to build

correct and elegant software

Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages, learning

these languages should be considered a side benefit of the course, not the main

goal.

Special Course Designation

None

The 2nd edition is no longer in print. The new, 3rd edition (ISBN 9780128154120) is

available in the bookstore. Using either edition will be fine for readings. Assigned

readings are based on 2nd edition.

Google Drive Folder

Notes:

Students must use TAMU credentials to access the shared folder.  Allow 24 hours

after registering for the class to access this shared drive.

Grading Policy

The course grade is based on the following components:

Homeworks: in forms of writing and/or coding: 5 assignments: 20% of grade.

Each will carry a different weight.

Quizzes: 4 online quizzes and unannounced, in-class pop quizzes: 20% of

grade. Online quizzes are conducted online using Respondus Lockdown

Browser. All quizzes are open-note only. Each will carry a different weight.

Exams: 1 midterm exam (30% of grade), and 1 final (non-cumulative) exam

(30% of grade). A double-sided 8.5×11 cheat sheet with font size no smaller

than 11pt if typed, or handwritten, may be used in the exam.

grade. Online quizzes are conducted online using Respondus Lockdown

Browser. All quizzes are open-note only. Each will carry a different weight.

Exams: 1 midterm exam (30% of grade), and 1 final (non-cumulative) exam

(30% of grade). A double-sided 8.5×11 cheat sheet with font size no smaller

than 11pt if typed, or handwritten, may be used in the exam.

Course Grading Scale:  F < 50%  ≤  D < 65%  ≤  C < 78%  ≤  B < 90%  ≤  A

Final grade boundaries are determined by the lowest earned score in each

grade bracket, not whole-numbered percentages. In other words, the

minimum for any grade is defined by the score of the person at the bottom of

that grade bracket. Decimal proximity to a round number does not result in

an adjustment.

The grade assigned is not on a curve. In this course, no curve will be given.

There will be no extra-credit assignments.

The grades will be audited randomly at any point. This may result in

increased/decreased scores for released grades. Affected students will be

An example: each bar represents the lowest-

scoring person in each grade bracket. If your

final score is below a bar, you are in the

lower grade

notified.

Class Participation:

This course strictly enforces the University’s Attendance Policy, which states

The University views class attendance and participation as an

individual student responsibility.

Students are expected to attend class and to complete all

assignments.

No credit will be awarded for class attendance. If students miss class, it is their

responsibility to catch up with the materials and announcements. The course staff

will not repeat announcements that were made in class.

Attendance Certification:

As required by Federal aid regulations, the University requires an attendance

certification of each student having begun engagement in an academically related

activity. This course uses submissions of assignments, quizzes, homeworks as

evidence to satisfy the required documentation. The attendance certification is due

approximately at the end of the 3rd week. Students are hereby notified that by the

due date - specifically  01/29/2026 5:00 PM , if students have zero submissions, they

will not be certified of this course’s attendance. There will be no warning or

communication should a student be at risk of not being certified. Failure of this

certification might negatively impact students’ financial aid.

Regrading:

Regrading can be requested for any graded materials. The guidelines are:

The request must be made on Gradescope and within a specified window.

Outside of this window, no requests will be accepted. No requests via email

will be accepted. No exceptions will be made.

The request must include a reasonable justification and/or documentation.

Regrading:

Regrading can be requested for any graded materials. The guidelines are:

The request must be made on Gradescope and within a specified window.

Outside of this window, no requests will be accepted. No requests via email

will be accepted. No exceptions will be made.

The request must include a reasonable justification and/or documentation.

Non-code submissions (e.g., writing assignments, exams): If the handwriting is

not legible, what the graders guess is final, and the request will be denied.

The following kinds of submissions will get 0 without grading, and regrading

will be denied:

Disorganized, messy submissions.

Wrong submissions

Submissions that did not follow the instructions (e.g., submitting

different file types, not matching the assignment outline with the

Gradescope submission, using banned libraries).

Uncompilable programming assignments on Gradescope. “It works on

my machine” argument will be ignored.

Makeup Exam Policy

Unless due to excused absences, no makeup exam will be given. If an excused

absence is expected, students should contact the instructor as soon as possible

(with documentation). Makeup exams will be different from the original exams.

If a student misses an exam due to an excused absence, the student must take the

makeup exam on the designated date and time:

Midterm: Monday, 3/2/26 at 6:30 PM in PETR 226

Final: Monday, 5/4/26 at 6:30 PM in PETR 226

Discuss with the instructor directly if rescheduling is required.

Incomplete Grade Policy

Per Student Rule 10, the instructor shall give an incomplete grade only in cases due

to an authorized absence or other causes beyond the control of the student, such

as illness or personal emergency. No exceptions will be made. Students are

required to furnish appropriate documentation. Under no circumstances can the “I”

grade be given to a student who wishes to:

Do additional work after the course deadline to improve a grade

Redo the coursework in a subsequent semester to improve the current grade

Awarding an incomplete is always at the discretion of the instructor.

Deviations from the grading policy

The grade for individual assignments and the course is assigned to each student

based on concrete, quantifiable, objectively evaluated submissions and exams.

Grade is not given to students. Hence, in the interest of fairness and consistency,

requests for grade increases that are inconsistent with the stated grading scale are

denied.

Here is an incomplete list of hypothetical requests:

(I feel) I do not deserve [the grade/score] because of [reason(s)]

(I feel) The (grading/course) rubric is unbalanced due to [reason(s)]

I spend [huge number]% of my time on X which accounts for only [small

requests for grade increases that are inconsistent with the stated grading scale are

denied.

Here is an incomplete list of hypothetical requests:

(I feel) The (grading/course) rubric is unbalanced due to [reason(s)]

I spend [huge number]% of my time on X which accounts for only [small

number]% of the grade, [elaborate on how the grading should be here]

With the grade lower than . . . , I will be [insert a consequence here]

I am really close to getting a . . .

I have a good GPA so far

I have never failed a class before.

I have never taken a course in this area before

I have never gotten a grade as low as . . . before

It doesn’t hurt to ask

I am about to graduate.

This course is too hard

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Submission of Assignments and Grade Posting:

All assignments must be turned in electronically on Gradescope. Email

submissions will not be accepted. Emails with submission attachments will be

deleted without response. No exceptions will be made.

Students are advised not to use Gradescope as a debugging tool, as the

autograder might be buggy. Students, therefore, should focus on debugging

locally.

Grade posting: The score shown in Gradescope will be imported into Canvas

as-is after the deadline (and regrading).

Assignment Deadlines and Late Penalty:

All assignments have established deadlines. Submitting a deliverable after the

deadline is late work. Late submission is allowed but will incur a late penalty. No late

work outside the grace period is accepted unless due to university-approved

absences. The course staff will not answer assignment questions during the grace

period.

Should deadline discrepancies arise between the syllabus, assignment

description PDF, and Gradescope: the deadline on Gradescope takes

precedence and is the official deadline.

Late submission will be graded as if it were on time, and then a late penalty

will be deducted (out of full credit). Late penalty will be calculated as follows:

Let h be the whole number of hours late (rounding up), the penalty is

h²/24 + h. That is, a 12-hour-late assignment will receive -18% (out of

100% — full credit) as a penalty; 1 day late, -48%. At 36 hours late, the

most one can score in an assignment is 10. At 39 hours late, the

will be deducted (out of full credit). Late penalty will be calculated as follows:

Let h be the whole number of hours late (rounding up), the penalty is

h²/24 + h. That is, a 12-hour-late assignment will receive -18% (out of

100% — full credit) as a penalty; 1 day late, -48%. At 36 hours late, the

most one can score in an assignment is 10. At 39 hours late, the

assignment receives no credit.

See the graph below:

Late penalty is waived in cases of university-approved absences

Excused absences: Students are required to furnish documentation for

an excused absence by default. When possible, notify the course staff

of excused absences before the deadline - not after. Refer to Student

Rule 7 for information about excused absences. This course will strictly

enforce Rule 7.

Computer crashes, malfunctions, and catastrophic loss of files are not valid

excuses for not submitting work on time (and thus request for the waiver of

the late penalty). Students are advised to turn in assignments early to avoid

any issues due to emergencies.

Students are responsible for making sure submissions are successfully

submitted on Gradescope. Unless due to global, public outages of

Gradescope, networking connectivity problems are not excuses for missing or

late Gradescope submissions.

Technology issues are not an excuse for missing a deadline. Make sure to

prepare the environments for assignments well in advance of deadlines, and

reach out to course staff early for help.

The instructor reserves the right to define makeup work for an excused absence,

which is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy.

Additional Course Information

Professionalism and Respect

Students are expected to treat the instructor and all other participants in the

course with courtesy and respect — refer to Student Rule 21. Comments to others

should be factual, constructive, and free from harassment. Unprofessional or

disrespectful conduct will not be tolerated. Warnings will not be given. Students will

need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive ways within the course.

Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions

as specified within the Student Rules.

Recording Statement

Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student

has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording.

This accommodation letter must be presented to the instructor in advance of any

Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions

as specified within the Student Rules.

Recording Statement

Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student

has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording.

This accommodation letter must be presented to the instructor in advance of any

recording being done. Students with permission to record classes are not

permitted to redistribute audio or video recordings of statements or comments

from the course to other individuals without the express permission of the faculty

member and of any students who are recorded.

Course Material Policy

Materials used in this course are subject to copyright. Students are not allowed to

reproduce, distribute, or post any lecture materials, assignments, exams, solutions,

etc., publicly. Students may take notes and make copies of course materials for

personal use. Students may not post course materials on commercial websites

such as Course Hero and Chegg. Doing so is a copyright violation and, in some

cases, may also be an academic integrity violation that will be dealt with accordingly.

AI Statement

Plagiarism - Academic Integrity

Plagiarism is one of the worst academic sins, for the plagiarist destroys trust among

colleagues and the purpose of learning. Plagiarism is commonly defined as passing

off as one’s own ideas, words, writings, code, etc., which belong to another person.

By this definition, in this course, a student commits plagiarism if copying the work

elsewhere, even with the permission of the author, and turns it in as one’s own.

The instructor encourages collaboration and the exchange of ideas among

students. Collaboration is important for facilitating learning, and peers often can be

a great resource. However, all assignments in this course are individual efforts. All

assignments must be fully completed by only the student to whom they are

assigned, without unauthorized aid. Any academic dishonesty will not be tolerated

and will be severely dealt with. Consequences include a zero grade for the

activity/assignment, course failure, and dismissal from the university, among others.

Refer to the University's Academic Integrity Statement and Policy. All involved

parties will be penalized equally.

Specifically, students may discuss the assignments at a high level (e.g.,

concepts, approaches) with friends but absolutely no actual solution sharing

(voluntarily or involuntarily) is allowed.

Code: Students are not allowed to use code found elsewhere (e.g., Google

searches, online repositories, past offerings of the course). Students also

must not attempt to circumvent the objective of an assignment (e.g., using a

concepts, approaches) with friends but absolutely no actual solution sharing

(voluntarily or involuntarily) is allowed.

Code: Students are not allowed to use code found elsewhere (e.g., Google

searches, online repositories, past offerings of the course). Students also

must not attempt to circumvent the objective of an assignment (e.g., using a

banned library or a function to bypass the goal of the assignment)

Non-code: The write-up of homeworks must be done individually and must

not be shared in any way or form.

No full or partial deliverables are allowed to be knowingly or unknowingly

released in the public domain even after the deadlines and after the course is

over. Students are solely responsible for securing all deliverables away from

the public.

Group review sessions for exams are allowed and encouraged.

Posting assignments to or copying solutions from unauthorized sources is

academic misconduct.

There have been past incidents where students used code from Chegg or

similar tutoring websites; the use of such resources is not allowed.

Complicity

Every student should understand that complicity – helping or attempting to help

another student commit an act of academic dishonesty – also constitutes academic

dishonesty and carries the same punishment as cheating and plagiarism.

In other words, if providing a solution to another student, even if that student does

not turn it in for credit, the provider has committed an act of academic dishonesty.

Occasionally, we find evidence of plagiarism after the assignments have been

graded. For example, a prior assignment may be flagged when a new detector

becomes available. For these reasons, you should treat your assignment grades as

provisional. Any prior submission that is later found to constitute a violation will be

reported to the Aggie Honor System Office (AHSO).

Use of Generative AI:

The use of AI to gain a greater understanding of a topic is a great use of its

capabilities. This is allowed. However, using AI to complete assignments is a poor

use of its capabilities, as it does not increase a student’s capabilities. This is

prohibited.

Code: While consulting AI tools is allowed, students are prohibited from

submitting AI-generated code for the assignments.

Writing: All non-code submissions must be generated by students

themselves. Students must not have another entity write any portion of an

assignment for them.

Campuswire

Campuswire will be the main communication and Q&A platform, not Canvas. The

class link and code to join will be provided in the first class meeting. If you drop the

class, please leave Campuswire. Interfering with or disrupting Campuswire will be

reported and result in disciplinary actions according to Student Rules.

assignment for them.

Campuswire

Campuswire will be the main communication and Q&A platform, not Canvas. The

class link and code to join will be provided in the first class meeting. If you drop the

class, please leave Campuswire. Interfering with or disrupting Campuswire will be

reported and result in disciplinary actions according to Student Rules.

Unless regarding personal matters (e.g., grade), questions are to be posted

on Campuswire first, for the benefit of other students. Students who wish to

discuss personal matters should see the course staff during office hours.

Students are encouraged to participate in the forum and help answer

questions. Campuswire is mainly made for students by students.

Students must never post codes or solutions on Campuswire for any reason.

If asking for implementation clarification/guide, describe what has been done

in pseudocode. Posts with codes/solutions will be deleted, and the poster

might be banned from Campuswire.

All posts to Campuswire must be public (visible to everyone) and un-

anonymous. Private posts or messages to the course staff will be deleted.

The order of getting answers is: Campuswire or in class, or office hours.

Emailing should be last.

Students must not treat Campuswire as a de-facto email system (e.g., make a

post visible to TA and instructor only and discuss code/solution of the

student). Those who violate will be banned from Campuswire.

The course staff will not check Campuswire during non-working hours, e.g.,

weekends and late hours.

In the past, students tended to wait until the due date and then panic and ask

questions. This is ineffective and discouraged. To enforce better behavior, the

course staff will not check Campuswire during the last 6 hours before the

deadline.

Respondus Lockdown Browser

Some assessments will be conducted online using Respondus Lockdown Browser

with Monitor - and will be referred to as quizzes. There is no cost to students to use

the software. Students are required to have a webcam, which may be built into the

computer or may be an external camera. The software is available for Mac, PC, and

Chromebooks. It is important to test LockDown Browser and the webcam before

using the application in a quiz. There will be an opportunity for students to practice

and set up the environment. Students must ensure that they have a functional

environment during the course to take quizzes. Unless due to extended excused

absences, no makeup is possible.

Note: the “Available Until” date and time is a hard limit, triggering a submission

automatically, and overriding any remaining time on the timer. Each quiz is not

longer than 1 hour.

University Library (Evans Annex) Tech Bar: Any Texas A&M student with an

environment during the course to take quizzes. Unless due to extended excused

absences, no makeup is possible.

Note: the “Available Until” date and time is a hard limit, triggering a submission

automatically, and overriding any remaining time on the timer. Each quiz is not

longer than 1 hour.

University Library (Evans Annex) Tech Bar: Any Texas A&M student with an

active NetID can check out electronics such as Chromebooks, chargers, and

webcams from the Tech Bar for free. Equipment reservations are available up

to seven days in advance. Walkups are also possible. For Chromebooks, use

https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/chromebooksAnnex ; for accessories, use

https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/computeraccessoriesAnnex

Respondus should be available for use on these Chromebooks as a Chrome

extension.

Alternatively, students may be able to take Respondus Lockdown Browser

exams in select computer labs on campus. See TAMU—Open Access Lab

(OAL) Computer Workstation Locations ( http://tx.ag/TAMUoal ) for more

information.

Course Announcements

Aside from Campuswire posts, the instructor will make class announcements via a

mailing list:  cs-csce314504-spring2026@lists.tamu.edu  - not Canvas. Students

should make sure they can receive emails from the mailing list. All emails to the

mailing list are assumed to be read after 12 hours. Allow 24 hours after registering

for the class for the roster to be synchronized with the mailing list. Past emails may

be found on the Google Group page.

How to succeed in this course (or any course):

Here are a few tips:

Attend class meetings regularly.

Take notes during lectures.

Read the assigned readings before the lecture and your notes after class.

Read the assigned readings again after class.

Ask questions and participate in discussions in class and on Campuswire.

Campuswire is FYBY - For You and By You.

Practice, practice, practice. For each credit hour, you are expected to spend 3

hours per week outside of class. Allow sufficient time to absorb the materials.

Study for exams. Group study/review for exams is allowed. Students in the

past have found group review sessions and crowd-sourcing practice tests and

review sheets helpful.

Complete assignments on time.

Visit the course staff during office hours to get questions answered.
